properties.raw_description: Pip identified his family name as Pirrip and his Christian name as Philip
Pip's infant tongue could not articulate his full names

Pip came to be called Pip
Pip identifies his family name as Pirrip based on his father's tombstone and his sister's information
Pip reflects on never having seen his father or mother
Pip notes that he has never seen a likeness of his parents due to the absence of photographs
Pip's first fancies about his parents are derived from their tombstones

Pip perceived his mother as freckled and sickly
Five little stone lozenges were arranged in a neat row beside their grave
The narrator reflects on the memory of five little brothers
The narrator believes that the five little brothers were born on their backs with their hands in their trousers-pockets
The narrator describes the five little brothers as having given up trying to get a living early in life
The narrator gained a vivid impression of the identity of things
Pip found out that the bleak place overgrown with nettles was the churchyard
Pip realized that Philip Pirrip and Georgiana were dead and buried
Pip realized that Alexander, Bartholomew, Abraham, Tobias, and Roger were also dead and buried

Pip felt afraid of it all

The little devil was threatened
A fearful man appeared, all in coarse grey, with a great iron on his leg
The fearful man had no hat, broken shoes, and an old rag tied round his head
The fearful man had been soaked in water, smothered in mud, lamed by stones, cut by flints, stung by nettles, and torn by briars

The fearful man's teeth chattered in his head

Pip pleaded in terror

Our village is a mile or more from the church

The man turned Pip upside down
The man emptied Pip's pockets
Pip's pockets contained nothing but a piece of bread
The church went head over heels before Pip
Pip saw the steeple under his feet
Pip was seated on a high tombstone

The man ate the bread ravenously
The man licked his lips

Pip felt timid

The convict came closer to Pip's tombstone
